problem_statement.Title: AssertionRewriter crashes when first top-level expression is a non-string constant (e.g. number)
problem_statement.Description: During test collection/import, pytest's assertion rewriter treats the first top-level ast.Constant expression as the module docstring even when that constant is not a string (for example, a numeric literal). That non-string value is forwarded into a function that expects a string and the import/collection aborts with a TypeError.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) AssertionRewriter.run (src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py) mis-identifies the module docstring: the code assumes that if the first top-level statement is an ast.Expr whose value is ast.Constant, then that Constant's .value should be treated as the module docstring. The code assigns doc = item.value.value and immediately calls is_rewrite_disabled(doc) without verifying the runtime type of item.value.value. (See rewrite.py:668 and rewrite.py:675-682.)

2) AssertionRewriter.is_rewrite_disabled (src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py) assumes it receives a string and uses the 'in' operator on its argument: def is_rewrite_disabled(docstring: str) -> bool: return "PYTEST_DONT_REWRITE" in docstring. Passing a non-str (e.g. int) into this function raises TypeError ("argument of type 'int' is not iterable"). (See rewrite.py:744-746.)

3) The combination of (1) and (2) leads to the observed crash during assertion rewriting: rewrite_asserts/_rewrite_test call AssertionRewriter.run and thus will surface the TypeError for modules whose first top-level expression is a non-string Constant. (See rewrite.py:340-343 and rewrite.py:401-403.)

4) Note on initial doc retrieval and intended contract: the code first sets doc = getattr(mod, "docstring", None) (rewrite.py:668). The practical problem occurs when the code subsequently promotes item.value.value (the AST Constant's value) into doc without type-checking. The intended contract for a module docstring is that it is either a str or None; standard library helper ast.get_docstring returns only str or None for module docstrings. This contract is why downstream code (is_rewrite_disabled and checks for the sentinel string) operates on the assumption that the docstring is a string or None.
StepsToReproduce:
Create a test module whose first top-level statement is a bare numeric literal, for example a file test_num_doc.py containing:

123

def test_dummy():
    assert True
Run pytest to collect that file, for example: pytest -q or pytest -k test_num_doc
Observe the collection/import error (TypeError) before tests run
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Pytest should not crash during import/collection when a module's first top-level expression is a non-string constant. Only string values should be treated as the module docstring; checks for the sentinel string "PYTEST_DONT_REWRITE" should only be applied to string docstrings. If the module has no docstring or the docstring does not contain the sentinel, assertion rewriting proceeds and tests are collected normally.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Pytest raises a TypeError during import/collection: "TypeError: argument of type 'int' is not iterable". The traceback shows AssertionRewriter.run calling is_rewrite_disabled with a non-string (int) value when the module's first top-level expression is a numeric literal, aborting test collection.

problem_statement.Title: pmxbot.logging module object duplicated when using import-mode=importlib; initializer side-effects not visible to tests
problem_statement.Description: After switching import-mode to importlib and reorganizing tests, core.initialize() executes and appears to set pmxbot.logging.Logger.store, but the tests observe a different pmxbot.logging module object. As a result the initializer's side-effects are not visible to the test module object and tests fail with AttributeError.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Duplicate-module assumption in tests: tests/unit/test_commands.py:TestCommands.setup_class (the test observed in the bug report) assumes a single canonical pmxbot.logging module object is used by all importers and that setting attributes on that module (e.g., Logger.store) will be visible to the test's imported binding. (Location: tests/unit/test_commands.py:TestCommands.setup_class)

2) Initializer/loader created or executed a non-canonical module object: pmxbot.core.initialize (as invoked by the test) executed code that performed module creation/loading in a way that resulted in a distinct module object identity from the one the test code imported. The available runtime evidence (PDB excerpt in the original bug report) shows two module objects whose identities differ; this is consistent with a loader creating/executing a module object that was not the canonical sys.modules[name] object. The exact implementation in core.initialize could not be inspected in this environment; the assertion that initialize used a spec- or path-based loader is an inference from the symptom. (Location: pmxbot.core.initialize — inferred loader usage)

3) Logger.store visibility assumption: code that sets Logger.store assumes there is only one pmxbot.logging module object and that setting an attribute on that module will be visible to all importers. Evidence shows the Logger class object exists in both module objects but the attribute Logger.store exists only on the module object created/modified by initialize and not on the module object used by the test. (Location: pmxbot.logging (Logger), and the site within core.initialize where Logger.store is set — exact line not available in this inspection)

4) General loader anti-pattern / canonical-registration expectation: the root pattern that produces the symptom is creating/executing module objects by path/spec without ensuring the created object is the canonical module object registered in sys.modules under the module's canonical name. The diagnostic expectation (relationship) is: the canonical module object for a module name must be the object at sys.modules[name]; if code uses importlib.util.module_from_spec/spec_from_file_location and executes the created module object via spec.loader.exec_module(module), the created module must be the canonical object (i.e., it must be the object stored at sys.modules[name] before execution) for side-effects to be visible to other importers. The observed mismatch indicates this expectation was violated in one or more dynamic loading sites invoked by initialize or test setup. (Location: any dynamic loader or helper invoked by core.initialize, and any other ad-hoc loader sites in the codebase such as plugin loaders or test import helpers)
StepsToReproduce: Check out pmxbot at the commit referenced in the original report with import-mode set to importlib and the tests reorganized as described.
Run the affected unit tests (for example: pytest tests/unit/test_commands.py or the full test suite).
Observe failure in TestCommands.setup_class when calling logging.Logger.store.message(...) raising AttributeError: type object 'Logger' has no attribute 'store'.

In a debugger, inspect the logging module object and sys.modules['pmxbot.logging'] and confirm that sys.modules['pmxbot.logging'] is not the same object as the module bound in the test (i.e., identity comparison is False).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: core.initialize()'s side-effects (including setting pmxbot.logging.Logger.store) are visible to the test code that imports pmxbot.logging; there is a single canonical pmxbot.logging module object such that logging.Logger.store.message(...) succeeds in tests.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Two distinct module objects for pmxbot.logging exist at runtime. The initializer sets Logger.store on one module object, but the test code imports/binds a different pmxbot.logging module object; as a result, logging.Logger.store is missing in the test's module object and the test raises AttributeError.

problem_statement.Title: Assertion rewriter shows generator object for all()/any(generator expr) leading to unhelpful failure messages
problem_statement.Description: When an assertion uses all(...) or any(...) with a generator expression argument, the pytest assertion rewriter displays the generator object (<generator object ...>) instead of the evaluated contents or the failing element, producing an unhelpful failure message.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect assumption: generic_visit's default handling is sufficient for all expression types, including ast.GeneratorExp. Location: src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py - AssertionRewriter.generic_visit. Evidence: generic_visit assigns the expression to a temporary (self.assign(node)) and returns an explanation that calls display(res); this results in the generator object's saferepr (e.g. '<generator object ...>') being shown instead of element-level information (see lines ~808-813).

2) Incorrect assumption: call visitors need not special-case generator expressions or detect calls to builtins that take generator arguments. Location: src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py - AssertionRewriter.visit_Call_35. Evidence: visit_Call_35 unconditionally visits each call argument (for arg in call.args: res, expl = self.visit(arg)), so a generator expression argument is handled by generic_visit and ends up shown as the generator object (see lines ~967-992).

3) Incorrect assumption: legacy call-handling is already covered. Location: src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py - AssertionRewriter.visit_Call_legacy. Evidence: visit_Call_legacy mirrors visit_Call_35 (visiting each arg with self.visit) and likewise does not special-case generator expressions; both call-handling paths therefore encode the same assumption and must be considered equally (see lines ~1000-1028).

4) Incorrect assumption: referring to builtins by an unqualified ast.Name (e.g., ast.Name('list')) is safe. Location: src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py - AssertionRewriter.builtin and top-of-module import injection. Evidence: the rewriter injects an alias @py_builtins and provides builtin(name) which returns an attribute on @py_builtins (see lines ~672-705 and ~757-761); this indicates unqualified names may be shadowed by user code and that transformations that insert calls to builtins must account for that.

5) Incorrect assumption (design implication): diagnostic-only changes can always be made without changing program behavior. Location: src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py - visit_Call_35 / visit_Call_legacy (design implication). Evidence: materializing a GeneratorExp (for the purpose of richer diagnostics) would force full evaluation and thus change timing, memory characteristics, and side-effect ordering of the program; this behavioral trade-off is a real consequence of any minimal change aimed at improving the assertion message.

6) Investigation / coverage gaps discovered during analysis (equal-weight findings):
   - The visitor currently does not distinguish between different AST shapes for the callee (e.g., ast.Name('all') versus ast.Attribute(...)). Any diagnosis or transformation that aims to affect only builtin all/any must consider these AST shapes and the possibility that 'all' or 'any' could be shadowed or accessed via attributes (noted but not validated by the investigation).
   - The analysis did not audit other lazy-iterator producing constructs (e.g., map(), filter(), itertools functions) to determine whether they produce the same unhelpful output pattern in asserts; this remains unverified.
   - The investigation did not run a full end-to-end example through visit_Assert to confirm the exact final formatted assertion message after any transformation; the exact user-visible output (template text and variable names) was not confirmed in an end-to-end test run.
StepsToReproduce: Run pytest on the test file (e.g., `pytest test_file.py`).
Observe the assertion failure output for the all(...) / any(...) assertion.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When an all(...) or any(...) assertion using a generator expression fails, the pytest assertion output should show useful information about the failing element(s) or the evaluated contents (e.g., the specific item/value that caused the assertion to fail), similar in informativeness to the equivalent for-loop assertion.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior:
pytest prints an unhelpful failure message showing the generator object, e.g.:

E   assert False
E    +  where False = all(<generator object ...>)

which does not reveal which item failed or the evaluated values.

problem_statement.Title: pytest --fixtures and --fixtures-per-test omit fixture scope in listings
problem_statement.Description: When running pytest --fixtures or pytest --fixtures-per-test, the printed fixture listings do not include each fixture's scope (session/package/module/class/function); only the fixture name (and path in verbose mode) are shown.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) src/_pytest/python.py:_showfixtures_main formats fixture entries using only the fixture name (or "name -- path" in verbose mode) and does not reference the FixtureDef.scope attribute. Example from source: in _showfixtures_main the printed funcargspec is created by
    if verbose > 0:
        funcargspec = "%s -- %s" % (argname, bestrel)
    else:
        funcargspec = argname
   (src/_pytest/python.py lines ~1345-1348). This omission causes the global fixture listing (pytest --fixtures) to not show scope.

2) src/_pytest/python.py:_show_fixtures_per_test (write_fixture nested function) formats per-test fixture entries the same way and likewise does not reference fixture.scope. Example: write_fixture constructs funcargspec with
    if verbose > 0:
        funcargspec = "{} -- {}".format(argname, bestrel)
    else:
        funcargspec = argname
   (src/_pytest/python.py lines ~1261-1265). This causes pytest --fixtures-per-test to omit fixture scopes.

3) src/_pytest/fixtures.py:FixtureDef (and PseudoFixtureDef) store a scope string and provide canonical scope names. Evidence: PseudoFixtureDef defines a scope attribute (attr) and FixtureDef.__init__ sets self.scope and computes self.scopenum; canonical scopes are defined as
    scopes = "session package module class function".split()
   (src/_pytest/fixtures.py lines ~42-46, ~829-835, ~675-676). The scope data is present and canonical, so the omission in the printing code is an omission of available information rather than lack of data.

4) Some tests inspect fixture output using permissive fnmatch patterns that match substrings (e.g. patterns containing '*' such as "*tmpdir*--*tmpdir.py*" or other wildcard-based checks in testing/python/fixtures.py). These tests implicitly assume the existing printed format (name and optional path) and could require review if the printed format is changed to include scope; however, many of the current assertions use wildcards and may still match after inserting a scope token.
StepsToReproduce: Have a test project with at least one fixture defined (any scope).
Run: pytest --fixtures and inspect the fixture listing output; observe that each fixture shows only its name (and path in verbose output) but not the fixture scope.
Run: pytest --fixtures-per-test and inspect per-test fixture listings; observe that fixture scopes are also omitted there.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: pytest --fixtures and pytest --fixtures-per-test should list each fixture and include its scope string (one of: session, package, module, class, function) in the human-readable fixture listing so users can see the scope alongside the fixture name and any path information.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Fixture listings printed by pytest --fixtures and pytest --fixtures-per-test show only the fixture name or "name -- path" (in verbose mode) and do not include the fixture's scope; scope values are present on FixtureDef objects but are not printed.

problem_statement.Title: DEFAULT_LOG_FORMAT omits logger name (%(name)s) — hardcoded format in src/_pytest/logging.py
problem_statement.Description: The repository-wide default log format constant (DEFAULT_LOG_FORMAT) in src/_pytest/logging.py hardcodes a filename-first, fixed-width layout and omits the logger name (%(name)s). As a result, formatted log lines lack module/logger context, making it harder to identify the source module in large projects.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) DEFAULT_LOG_FORMAT hardcoded value (src/_pytest/logging.py:DEFAULT_LOG_FORMAT) encodes the incorrect assumption that the base filename + line number (with fixed-width alignment) are sufficient identifiers and that the logger name is not required. Evidence: DEFAULT_LOG_FORMAT = "%(filename)-25s %(lineno)4d %(levelname)-8s %(message)s" (src/_pytest/logging.py:18). This constant is the repository's default used for CLI defaults and plugin formatters.

2) ColoredLevelFormatter compatibility requirement (src/_pytest/logging.py:ColoredLevelFormatter.LEVELNAME_FMT_REGEX). The formatter locates a "%(levelname)" token with an optional width specifier (regex r"%\(levelname\)([+-]?\d*s)") and substitutes a colorized levelname fragment. Incorrect assumption: arbitrary removal or alteration of a "%(levelname)...s" token could prevent the formatter from finding and colorizing the level name. Evidence: LEVELNAME_FMT_REGEX = re.compile(r"%\(levelname\)([+-]?\d*s)") and use of that match in ColoredLevelFormatter.__init__ (src/_pytest/logging.py:36, 46–63).

3) DEFAULT_LOG_FORMAT is used as the authoritative default for multiple formatting entry points (LoggingPlugin and pytest options). Incorrect assumption encoded: a single constant change will affect all default formatter outputs without other indirections. Evidence: pytest_addoption registers --log-format and --log-file-format with default=DEFAULT_LOG_FORMAT (src/_pytest/logging.py:109–113, 154–158); LoggingPlugin.__init__ constructs self.formatter = logging.Formatter(get_option_ini(config, "log_format"), ...) and self.log_file_formatter = logging.Formatter(self.log_file_format, datefmt=self.log_file_date_format) so the constant influences captured logs, live-logging, and log-file formatting (src/_pytest/logging.py:396–399, 403–409). _setup_cli_logging also derives its format from the same config keys and chooses ColoredLevelFormatter when the levelname pattern is present (src/_pytest/logging.py:440–452).

4) Tests exercise the old literal format but are not coupled to the constant. A test defines the old literal explicitly and asserts exact formatted output, so tests exist that expect the prior layout but do not import DEFAULT_LOG_FORMAT directly. Evidence: testing/logging/test_formatter.py defines logfmt = "%(filename)-25s %(lineno)4d %(levelname)-8s %(message)s" and asserts exact output (testing/logging/test_formatter.py:9, 27–36).

5) Investigation omissions affecting confidence in making a default change: the agent did not run the full test-suite with an updated DEFAULT_LOG_FORMAT, did not inspect the CPython logging.Formatter internals beyond repository usage, and did not search for external plugins or third-party tooling that might parse formatted log text. These remain outstanding validation tasks.
StepsToReproduce: Add or use a module that logs a message (e.g., a DEBUG log in utils.py).
Run pytest with default logging settings (no custom --log-format), e.g. `pytest`.
Observe the formatted log lines produced by pytest: they begin with the base filename and line number and do not include the logger/module name.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Default-formatted log lines should include the logger name (module name) so messages identify their source module (user-visible behavior).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Log lines formatted with the current default look like: "utils.py                   114 DEBUG    (0.000) SELECT ..." or "multipart.py               604 DEBUG    Calling on_field_start with no data" — they start with filename and lineno and do not show the logger name/module.

problem_statement.Title: str(ExceptionInfo) (pytest.raises context) truncates multi-line exception messages to first line
problem_statement.Description: Printing the pytest.raises context variable (an _pytest._code.ExceptionInfo instance) via str(excinfo) yields a file:lineno prefix plus only the first line of a multi-line exception message instead of the full message.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) ExceptionInfo.__str__ (src/_pytest/_code/code.py:537-543): constructs a ReprFileLocation using self.exconly() and returns str(loc). By delegating to ReprFileLocation, ExceptionInfo.__str__ exposes the behavior of that formatter instead of directly returning the full exception text.

2) ReprFileLocation.toterminal (src/_pytest/_code/code.py:941-956): explicitly truncates its message at the first newline before emitting the file:lineno: message line (implementation: i = msg.find("\n"); if i != -1: msg = msg[:i]). This enforces a single-line summary for file:lineno output and is the direct cause of losing subsequent lines when a multi-line message is passed through it.

3) ExceptionInfo.exconly (src/_pytest/_code/code.py:452-467): returns the full exception text (potentially containing embedded newlines) by joining format_exception_only output. The full multi-line message is therefore available from excinfo.exconly() and excinfo.value, but ExceptionInfo.__str__ passes that full text to ReprFileLocation which then truncates it.

4) Related/common uses (multiple locations): ReprFileLocation is used in other repr/reporting code paths that expect a short single-line file:lineno summary, e.g. ExceptionInfo._getreprcrash (src/_pytest/_code/code.py:472-476), FormattedExcinfo.repr_traceback_entry / ReprEntry.__str__ (src/_pytest/_code/code.py:668-693 and 935-938), and doctest reporting (src/_pytest/doctest.py:235-236). Because ReprFileLocation.toterminal enforces single-line output, any caller that constructs a ReprFileLocation with a potentially multi-line message will see the same truncation behavior.
StepsToReproduce: 1) Inside a normal try/except, raise an exception whose message contains embedded newlines and print(str(e)) in the except block. Example: try: raise LookupError('A\nB\nC') except LookupError as e: print(str(e)) — this prints the full multi-line message.

2) Use pytest.raises as a context manager to capture the same exception and print(str(excinfo)). Example: with pytest.raises(LookupError) as excinfo: raise LookupError('A\nB\nC') ; print(str(excinfo)) — this prints a file:lineno summary showing only the first line of the message.
3) Observe the difference: the first case shows all message lines; the pytest.raises captured ExceptionInfo shows only a file:lineno plus the first line.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Printing the pytest.raises context object (str(excinfo)) should present a file:lineno prefix followed by the full exception text including embedded newlines (equivalent to str(excinfo.value) for multi-line messages), so the full message is visible by default.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Printing str(excinfo) shows only a single-line summary consisting of file:lineno plus the first line of the exception message (for example: "<console>:3: LookupError: A"), with subsequent lines of a multi-line message omitted.

hints_text:
> Any chance the behavior could be changed to eliminate this gotcha?

What do you suggest?

Proxying through to the exceptions `__str__`?
Hi @fiendish,

Indeed this is a bit confusing.

Currently `ExceptionInfo` objects (which is `pytest.raises` returns to the context manager) implements `__str__` like this:

https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest/blob/9f8b566ea976df3a3ea16f74b56dd6d4909b84ee/src/_pytest/_code/code.py#L537-L542

I don't see much use for this, I would rather it didn't implement `__str__` at all and let `__repr__` take over, which would show something like:

```
<ExceptionInfo LookupError tb=10>
```

Which makes it more obvious that this is not what the user intended with `str(e)` probably.

So I think a good solution is to simply delete the `__str__` method.

Thoughts?

Also, @fiendish which Python version are you using?
> So I think a good solution is to simply delete the `__str__` method.

Makes sense to me.


Python 3.7.3

My ideal outcome would be for str(e) to act the same as str(e.value), but I can understand if that isn't desired.
> My ideal outcome would be for str(e) to act the same as str(e.value), but I can understand if that isn't desired.

I understand, but I think it is better to be explicit here, because users might use `print(e)` to see what `e` is, assume it is the exception value, and then get confused later when it actually isn't (an `isinstance` check or accessing `e.args`).
+1 for deleting the current `__str__` implementation
-1 for proxying it to the underlying `e.value`

the `ExceptionInfo` object is not the exception and anything that makes it look more like the exception is just going to add to the confusion

problem_statement.Title: pytest assertion output shows integer ordinals for bytes comparisons (e.g. "first extra item: 52")
problem_statement.Description: When pytest reports diffs for byte-string comparisons it prints integer ordinals for byte elements (e.g. "first extra item: 52") because bytes are treated as generic sequences and their indexed elements are ints, producing confusing assertion messages.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) src/_pytest/assertion/util.py:assertrepr_compare — the dispatch assumes only str is "text": istext(x) is implemented as isinstance(x, str) (istext definition). assertrepr_compare uses this predicate to decide whether to call the text diff routine (_diff_text) or the sequence diff routine (_compare_eq_sequence). Because bytes are not instances of str, they do not satisfy istext and are dispatched as generic sequences (see assertrepr_compare == branch).

2) src/_pytest/assertion/util.py:_compare_eq_sequence — the sequence-diff code assumes indexed sequence elements are representable as their original element type. It formats the message using saferepr(left[len_right]) or saferepr(right[len_left]) and the template "%s contains %d more items, first extra item: %s". For bytes (and other binary sequences that yield integer elements when indexed), right[len(left)] (or left[len(right)]) is an int (the byte ordinal), so saferepr prints e.g. "52" — producing the integer-ordinal message rather than a readable byte/escape representation.

3) src/_pytest/_io/saferepr.py:SafeRepr / saferepr — saferepr returns a size-limited repr(value) and does not special-case "byte-element-int" values. Therefore, when it is given an int it correctly returns the integer representation (e.g. "52"). This confirms the formatting choice is a consequence of the caller providing an int rather than a human-readable byte sequence.

4) src/_pytest/assertion/util.py:_diff_text — this routine already supports both text and bytes: its docstring states it handles "text or bytes" and it converts bytes into readable text using escape_for_readable_diff before producing a diff. _diff_text also contains the leading/trailing identical-character skip threshold logic (the i > 42 check and i -= 10 adjustment) which affects the context shown in long diffs.

5) src/_pytest/assertion/util.py:assertrepr_compare ("not in" branch) — the same istext-only check is used for the "not in" branch (if istext(left) and istext(right): explanation = _notin_text(...)), so bytes "not in" cases also avoid the readable-text path and receive the sequence/iterable fallback instead.

6) Related binary sequence types (bytearray/memoryview) — _diff_text only converts instances of bytes (isinstance(..., bytes)). Other binary sequence types such as bytearray are treated as sequences by assertrepr_compare and, when indexed, yield integer elements as well; thus they share the same root cause pattern (they will also result in ordinal ints being printed by _compare_eq_sequence). This is a distinct, related location to consider because _diff_text's bytes conversion is narrow (bytes only).

7) Tests gap and coverage nuance — there are tests that exercise bytes appearing in assertion messages (e.g. testing/test_assertrewrite.py: test_assertion_messages_bytes) and many sequence-diff tests for non-bytes sequences (e.g. testing/test_assertion.py). However, I did not find a regression test that asserts the human-readable bytes==bytes equality diff (i.e., a test that would fail if the equality-diff output showed an integer ordinal like "52" rather than a readable byte/escape diff).
StepsToReproduce: Run pytest on that file (e.g. pytest test_b.py).
Observe the assertion output that reports an integer ordinal for the extra byte (e.g. 'first extra item: 52').
(Optional) Repeat with assert b"" == b"1" to observe 'first extra item: 49'.)
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When two byte strings differ, pytest should display a human-readable byte-string diff (showing the differing bytes as readable byte characters/escapes) rather than printing integer ordinals for byte elements.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior:
Pytest prints integer ordinals for byte elements in sequence-typed diffs. Example output:

    def test_b():
>       assert b"" == b"42"
E       AssertionError: assert b'' == b'42'
E         Right contains more items, first extra item: 52
E         Full diff:
E         - b''
E         + b'42'
E         ?   ++

And for b'1':

    def test_b():
>       assert b"" == b'1'
E       AssertionError: assert b'' == b'1'
E         Right contains more items, first extra item: 49
E         Full diff:
E         - b''
E         + b'1'
E         ?   +

hints_text: hmmm yes, this ~kinda makes sense as `bytes` objects are sequences of integers -- we should maybe just omit the "contains more items" messaging for bytes objects?

problem_statement.Title: Pytest JUnit XML <testsuite> missing timestamp and hostname attributes
problem_statement.Description: When pytest generates JUnit XML (via --junitxml), the <testsuite> element does not include the timestamp and hostname attributes that are commonly present in JUnit reports.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Missing testsuite attributes at the single writer location: the JUnit XML writer constructs the <testsuite> element without timestamp and hostname. Location: src/_pytest/junitxml.py, class LogXML, method pytest_sessionfinish. Evidence: the Junit.testsuite(...) call passes only name, errors, failures, skipped, tests and time and omits timestamp and hostname.

2) The writer computes suite duration but does not serialize a timestamp string: pytest_sessionfinish computes suite_time_delta from self.suite_start_time and suite_stop_time but does not compute or attach a timestamp attribute to the testsuite element. Location: src/_pytest/junitxml.py, pytest_sessionstart (sets self.suite_start_time) and pytest_sessionfinish (uses suite_start_time to compute duration but does not produce a timestamp string).

3) Missing hostname computation: the module imports time but contains no hostname retrieval (no socket.gethostname() or equivalent) and does not provide a hostname attribute on the testsuite element. Location: src/_pytest/junitxml.py (top-level imports and pytest_sessionfinish).

4) Use-of-suite_start_time assumes it exists: pytest_sessionfinish uses self.suite_start_time directly (suite_time_delta = suite_stop_time - self.suite_start_time), which encodes the assumption that pytest_sessionstart has run and set suite_start_time on all execution paths. Location: src/_pytest/junitxml.py, LogXML.pytest_sessionfinish and pytest_sessionstart.

5) Tests do not assert presence of these attributes: existing junit output tests exercise many testsuite/testcase attributes but do not assert a testsuite timestamp or hostname, so the absence of these attributes is not covered by current tests. Location: testing/test_junitxml.py.

6) Unverified consumer/format expectations: there is no canonical timestamp format or timezone/precision requirement encoded in the repository for testsuite timestamps. The codebase does not specify how a timestamp should be formatted (for example, whether it must be an ISO-like localtime string, include timezone info, or include fractional seconds), so any assumed format would be an unverified consumer expectation. Location: investigation findings (no timestamp-format specification found in repo).
StepsToReproduce: Create a minimal test file (for example test_sample.py with one passing and one failing test).
Run pytest with JUnit XML output: pytest --junitxml=out.xml
Open out.xml and inspect the <testsuite> element: verify it does not include timestamp="..." or hostname="..." attributes.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The generated JUnit XML <testsuite> element includes a timestamp attribute with a value in the form YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SS (or otherwise documented ISO-like format) and a hostname attribute containing the machine's hostname, in addition to the existing attributes (name, tests, failures, errors, skipped, time).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The generated JUnit XML <testsuite> element only contains attributes name, tests, failures, errors, skipped and time (time formatted as '%.3f'), and is missing timestamp and hostname.

problem_statement.Title: pytest --collect-only has no one-character shortcut
problem_statement.Description: The --collect-only CLI option is registered only with long-form names and has no single-character alias; users must type the full long option and help output shows no one-character shortcut.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Missing short-option registration in option declaration: src/_pytest/main.py::pytest_addoption registers the collect-only option only with long names ("--collectonly", "--collect-only"); no short optstring is provided, which is why no one-character alias exists. (See: src/_pytest/main.py addoption call for collect-only.)

2) Option destination name depends on first long option: src/_pytest/config/argparsing.py::Argument derives the option destination (the attribute available at runtime as config.option.<dest>) using the formula dest = self._long_opts[0][2:].replace("-", "_"). Because the dest is computed from the first long option, changing the first long option or its order will change the runtime attribute name (currently "collectonly").

3) Short-option registration constraints enforced by parser/group code: src/_pytest/config/argparsing.py::OptionGroup._addoption_instance enforces that when shortupper is False (the normal addoption path), lowercase single-letter short options raise a ValueError("lowercase shortoptions reserved"). This means lowercase single-letter shortopts cannot be registered through the usual addoption API without using the special _addoption path or otherwise circumventing that check; uppercase single-letter shortopts are not rejected by that check.

4) Multiple runtime and reporting locations read the same dest: several runtime and reporting code paths read config.option.collectonly (the dest produced from the first long option). Notable locations observed in the codebase that rely on that dest are:
   - src/_pytest/main.py::pytest_runtestloop (if session.config.option.collectonly: return True)
   - src/_pytest/logging.py::pytest_runtestloop hook (checks session.config.option.collectonly)
   - src/_pytest/terminal.py (uses config.getoption("collectonly") to control printing of collected items and collection failures)
   - src/_pytest/pytester.py (test helper inline_genitems invokes the literal "--collect-only")
   Any alias that maps to the same dest will affect all these locations uniformly.

5) Tests and documentation reference the literal long option: many test files and doc examples invoke or mention "--collect-only" explicitly (for example testing/test_terminal.py::TestCollectonly, src/_pytest/pytester.py::inline_genitems, doc/en/example/pythoncollection.py). The investigation did not exhaustively search for tests or docs that assert exact help output formatting; such assertions (if present) would be affected by any change to help text or option presentation.

Each of the above is an equal-weight finding: the problem is that the option was declared without a short optstring in the single authoritative registration location (src/_pytest/main.py::pytest_addoption), the parser derives the runtime attribute name from the first long-form option, and the option-group/parser code enforces constraints on which single-letter short options can be added via the standard API. Multiple runtime/reporting locations and tests/docs depend on the existing dest/option name.
StepsToReproduce: Run `pytest -h` (help) and inspect the entry for collect-only; observe only the long option forms are shown (no single-character alias).
Attempt to invoke pytest with a one-character short flag for collect-only (e.g. `pytest -C`) and observe it is not accepted as an alias for --collect-only.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Help output shows a one-character alias for the collect-only option (for example: `-C, --collect-only`) and invoking that single-character alias behaves identically to `--collect-only` (pytest only collects tests and does not execute them).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: No one-character alias is available for the collect-only option; help shows only `--collectonly`/`--collect-only`, and users must use the full long option to trigger collection-only behavior.

problem_statement.Title: INTERNALERROR when formatting exception if object has failing __repr__/__getattribute__
problem_statement.Description: When pytest formats an exception and the object whose repr() is being rendered has a broken __repr__ or __getattribute__ that raises, pytest can raise an INTERNALERROR instead of producing a safe representation. The unsafe behavior arises while building a fallback representation in src/_pytest/_io/saferepr.py.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) src/_pytest/_io/saferepr.py::_try_repr_or_str — incorrect assumption: including the object instance as a format argument is safe when repr(obj) fails. The fallback uses '{}("{}")'.format(type(obj).__name__, obj) (see file lines 6–12), which passes obj into .format() and thus invokes obj.__format__/str(obj) (potentially calling user-defined __repr__/__getattribute__) and can raise while formatting the fallback.

2) src/_pytest/_io/saferepr.py::_format_repr_exception — incorrect assumption: accessing obj.__class__.__name__ is a safe, attribute-free operation. The helper composes "<[{} raised in repr()] {} object at 0x{:x}>" using obj.__class__.__name__ (see file lines 15–24). Accessing obj.__class__ performs instance attribute lookup and may invoke user-defined __getattribute__ and raise.

3) src/_pytest/_io/saferepr.py:SafeRepr.repr and SafeRepr.repr_instance — incorrect assumption: the formatting helper _format_repr_exception cannot itself raise. These methods catch exceptions from repr() and call _format_repr_exception(exc, x) (see file lines 45–61); because that helper performs instance-level operations, a second exception can escape and cause an INTERNALERROR instead of producing a safe repr.

4) Repository occurrences of the same pattern (audit gap) — other files contain uses of __class__.__name__ or similar instance-level attribute access that should be reviewed to determine whether they operate on arbitrary user objects and therefore encode the same unsafe assumption. Example occurrences found by repository search include: src/_pytest/_code/code.py (uses saferepr on exception objects), src/_pytest/capture.py, src/_pytest/main.py, src/_pytest/nodes.py, src/_pytest/outcomes.py, src/_pytest/reports.py. These occurrences were not exhaustively audited and merit review to confirm whether they are invoked on user-controlled objects.
StepsToReproduce: Create a test file containing:
class SomeClass:
def __getattribute__(self, attr):
raise
def __repr__(self):
raise
def test():
SomeClass().attr
Run pytest on the file (e.g., `pytest -q`).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Pytest should not raise an INTERNALERROR while formatting the exception. It should present a safe representation indicating that repr() failed (for example a short placeholder mentioning the type and id) and continue with normal test reporting, displaying the original exception that caused the failure.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Pytest raises an INTERNALERROR while building the exception representation. The traceback shows saferepr helpers invoking formatting or instance attribute access that re-enters user code (e.g., via obj.__format__ or obj.__getattribute__), leading to a secondary exception (for example "RuntimeError: No active exception to reraise") and preventing normal test reporting.

problem_statement.Title: Wrong path to test file when directory changed in fixture
problem_statement.Description: When a fixture (or code under test) changes the process working directory, pytest formats traceback file paths relative to that transient CWD. The failure lines shown in the test output become relative to the mutated directory (for example "../test_path_error.py:29") instead of the pytest invocation directory, preventing editors from jumping to the correct file location.
problem_statement.RootCause:
Multiple pieces of core formatting code assume the appropriate base for producing shortened/relative file paths is the current process working directory (the runtime CWD), and there is no existing mechanism in ExceptionInfo.getrepr/FormattedExcinfo for callers to provide the pytest invocation directory as an explicit base. These facts combine to cause traceback lines to be relativized against the transient CWD when tests or fixtures call os.chdir(). The relevant, equally-weighted findings are:

1) FormattedExcinfo._makepath shortens file paths using the process CWD as the base (src/_pytest/_code/code.py). The implementation calls py.path.local().bestrelpath(path) when abspath is False, which uses the current working directory for relativity. (See FormattedExcinfo._makepath where np = py.path.local().bestrelpath(path) is invoked.)

2) ExceptionInfo.getrepr currently has no parameter to accept a caller-supplied invocation/base path (src/_pytest/_code/code.py). The getrepr signature accepts showlocals, style, abspath, tbfilter, funcargs, truncate_locals, chain — there is no startpath or equivalent argument that would allow a caller to specify the stable pytest invocation directory as the base for path-shortening.

3) Core call sites that format exceptions call getrepr without any way to supply an invocation base (all call sites below call getrepr with no base argument):
   - Node._repr_failure_py (src/_pytest/nodes.py): calls excinfo.getrepr(...) without supplying any invocation/startpath parameter.
   - Config.notify_exception (src/_pytest/config/__init__.py): calls excinfo.getrepr(...) when reporting internal errors, with no invocation/startpath parameter.
   - ConftestImportFailure handling during early config in main (src/_pytest/config/__init__.py): exc_info.getrepr(style="short", chain=False) is called without a startpath.

4) A stable base for relativizing paths is available elsewhere in core: the session/config invocation directory is stored as session.startdir / config.invocation_dir (src/_pytest/main.py), and the terminal reporter already relativizes file system paths against that base (src/_pytest/terminal.py uses self.startdir.bestrelpath(fspath)). This demonstrates the intended UX: shorten paths relative to the pytest invocation directory rather than the transient process CWD.

Net effect: because the formatted-exception code shortens paths relative to py.path.local() (runtime CWD) and callers have no API-surface to provide the invocation directory, failure output shown by pytest can display file paths relative to a directory changed during test execution, producing paths (like "../test_path_error.py:29") that are incorrect/unusable for editors expecting paths relative to the pytest invocation directory.
StepsToReproduce: Create the file test_path_error.py in the project root using the provided fixture that creates and chdirs into directory 'ddd' and a failing test (as in the original report).
From the project root, run: pytest

Observe the failure output: the traceback line shows the test file path relative to the fixture-changed CWD (for example "../test_path_error.py:29: AssertionError") instead of relative to the pytest invocation directory ("test_path_error.py:29: AssertionError").
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When a test fails, pytest should display traceback file paths relative to the pytest invocation directory (the session/config invocation dir). For the provided repro, the failure line should read "test_path_error.py:29: AssertionError" so editors can open the file directly from the output.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: When a test or fixture changes the process working directory, pytest displays failure file paths relative to that transient CWD (for example "../test_path_error.py:29: AssertionError"), preventing editors from locating the file by the shown path.

problem_statement.Title: Expression-only cache key for string marker evaluation causes cross-module skip/xfail contamination
problem_statement.Description: pytest caches the evaluation result of string marker conditions (for example @pytest.mark.skipif("skip")) using only the expression text as the cache key. Because the boolean result of evaluating such an expression can depend on the module-level globals in which it is evaluated, using the expression text alone as the cache key can cause identical expressions in different modules to reuse the same cached result and produce incorrect skip/xfail decisions.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) cached_eval (src/_pytest/mark/evaluate.py) stores and looks up evaluation results using the expression string as the sole key (evalcache[expr]). This encodes the incorrect assumption that the evaluation result depends only on the expression text and not on the evaluation context (the globals dict d) provided to the evaluator. Evidence: cached_eval uses config._store.setdefault(evalcache_key, default) and then returns evalcache[expr] or sets evalcache[expr] after eval().

2) MarkEvaluator (_getglobals and _istrue in src/_pytest/mark/evaluate.py) builds and passes a per-item/module globals dict into cached_eval. _getglobals constructs a dict that includes interpreter/OS helpers and, when the item has an obj, merges the module globals via self.item.obj.__globals__. _istrue calls cached_eval(self.item.config, expr, d). Because cached_eval keys only on expr, per-module differences in globals are not taken into account when reusing cached results.

3) The eval cache is stored on the Config instance (config._store) under evalcache_key, so the expr-only cache is shared for that Config (test session/config). Because the cache key omits any module identity, identical expressions across different modules are conflated across the session, causing cross-module contamination of skip/xfail decisions.

4) Relationship required to avoid conflation: the evaluation cache must be partitioned by a stable identity derived from the evaluation globals in addition to the expression text. Concretely, a stable module identity can be derived from entries in the globals dict (for example, values such as __file__ and __name__ when present), so the true distinguishing key for caching is the expression plus the evaluation context identity (conceptually: (expr, module-identity)). Note: the exact choice of module-identity is a design decision; the investigation identified (__file__, __name__) as a practical identity that reflects a module's globals when present.

5) Edge case / uncovered gap: globals dicts passed to the evaluator are not guaranteed to contain stable identifying keys in all environments. The investigation did not exercise cases where module identity entries are absent or None, which is an uncovered gap to consider when designing any cache partitioning by module identity.
StepsToReproduce: Create test_module_1.py with a module-level variable skip = True and a test decorated with @pytest.mark.skipif("skip") that would fail if run.
Create test_module_2.py with a module-level variable skip = False and a test decorated with @pytest.mark.skipif("skip") that would fail if run.

problem_statement.Title: pytest --runxfail causes mark-based skip reports to show pytest's internal skipping.py location instead of test file
problem_statement.Description: When a test is skipped via @pytest.mark.skip or skipif, pytest normally reports the skip location as the test item's file and line. When pytest is run with --runxfail, the reported skip location instead points into pytest's internal skipping.py (the site of pytest.skip) rather than the test file/line.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1. Early runxfail short-circuit in src/_pytest/skipping.py::pytest_runtest_makereport — the hook contains an `elif item.config.option.runxfail: pass` branch that is evaluated before the branch which rewrites the report location for mark-originated skips. Because these are in the same elif chain, when runxfail is True the later rewrite branch is not reached.

2. The skip-location rewrite that is intended to make mark-based skips report the test item's file/line is present in the same function but is unreachable when the runxfail short-circuit is taken. Its exact transformation is: rep.longrepr = (str(filename), line + 1, reason) where (filename, line) = item.reportinfo()[:2].

3. The initial rep.longrepr for a skip is produced from the exception site by TestReport.from_item_and_call (src/_pytest/reports.py). For skip exceptions longrepr is set from excinfo._getreprcrash() and therefore points to the location where pytest.skip() was raised (i.e., inside skipping.py) unless another hook overwrites it.

4. The xfail-related branches (handling xfail.Exception and xfailed marks) and the runxfail check are located in the same elif chain in pytest_runtest_makereport, which makes the control-flow ordering significant: the current ordering encodes the assumption that enabling runxfail should prevent subsequent makereport transformations, but this also prevents the mark-driven skip-location rewrite from running.
StepsToReproduce:
Run pytest with skip reporting: pytest -rs
  - Observe: SKIPPED [1] test_it.py:<line>: unconditional skip (the reported location points to the test file).

Run pytest with runxfail enabled: pytest -rs --runxfail
  - Observe: SKIPPED [1] src/_pytest/skipping.py:<line>: unconditional skip (the reported location points into pytest's internal skipping.py / the site of pytest.skip()).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Mark-based skipped tests report the skip location as the test item's file and line (the test definition location). For example: SKIPPED [1] test_it.py:<line>: unconditional skip.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: When pytest is invoked with --runxfail, mark-based skipped tests report the skip location pointing into pytest's internal skipping.py (the location where pytest.skip is raised) instead of the test item's file/line.

problem_statement.Title: pytest 6: dynamically added xfail marker in test body is ignored; failing test reported as FAIL instead of XFAIL
problem_statement.Description: When an xfail marker is added dynamically inside the test body via request.node.add_marker(pytest.mark.xfail(...)), pytest 6 builds a TestReport that does not treat the failure as xfail and reports a FAIL. In pytest 5.x the same pattern produced an XFAIL.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) src/_pytest/skipping.py::pytest_runtest_makereport — incorrect assumption: makereport reads the stored xfail evaluation via item._store.get(xfailed_key, None) and treats that as the authoritative source for xfail state but does not re-run evaluate_xfail_marks(item) when the stored value is None. As a result, markers added during the call stage are not considered when deciding rep.wasxfail / rep.outcome.

2) src/_pytest/skipping.py::pytest_runtest_call — incorrect assumption: pre-call re-evaluation (this hookwrapper) runs before the test body and therefore cannot observe markers added inside the test body. Although it re-evaluates when item._store[xfailed_key] is None, that evaluation happens before the call stage executes, so it cannot detect marks added during execution.

3) src/_pytest/skipping.py::pytest_runtest_setup — incorrect assumption: initial setup-time evaluation stores an xfail result in item._store[xfailed_key] and thus contributes to the overall assumption that xfail evaluation need not be re-checked later. This early storage is part of the same pattern of relying on earlier evaluation rather than re-evaluating at report time.

4) src/_pytest/nodes.py::Node.add_marker and iter_markers — evidence location: Node.add_marker mutates node.own_markers and node.keywords immediately, and iter_markers/iter_markers_with_node yields node.own_markers for the node and its parents. Therefore, evaluate_xfail_marks(item) would detect marks added during the call stage if it were invoked after add_marker.

5) src/_pytest/skipping.py::evaluate_xfail_marks — this routine correctly inspects item.iter_markers(name="xfail") and returns an Xfail record (reason, run, strict, raises). Its correctness depends on being invoked at a time when item.iter_markers reflects markers added during the test body; the observed behavior arises because makereport does not invoke it when the stored value is None.
StepsToReproduce:
Create file test_foo.py with these contents:

import pytest


def test_xfail_test(request):
    mark = pytest.mark.xfail(reason="xfail")
    request.node.add_marker(mark)
    assert 0
Run pytest (example): pytest -rsx test_foo.py using pytest 6.0.0rc1 or 6.0.1rc0
Observe the test outcome reported as FAIL (not XFAIL).
For comparison, run the same file under pytest 5.4.3 and observe it reported as XFAIL.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When a test adds an xfail marker at runtime (inside the test body), a subsequent failure in that test should be reported as XFAIL (shown as xfailed) in the test output and summary.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: With pytest 6.0.0rc1/6.0.1rc0 the failing test that adds an xfail marker inside the test body is reported as FAIL; the TestReport does not mark it as xfailed.

problem_statement.Title: tmpdir creation fails when getpass.getuser() returns a username with path-separator characters (e.g. domain\user) on Windows
problem_statement.Description: pytest tmpdir/tmpdir_factory/tmp_path_factory use getpass.getuser() unchanged to build the basetemp directory name. If the returned username contains path-separator or filesystem-unsafe characters (for example a Windows domain username like "contoso\john_doe"), the constructed path becomes multi-component and directory creation fails on Windows with FileNotFoundError.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) src/_pytest/tmpdir.py:get_user assumes getpass.getuser() is a filesystem-safe single path component: get_user() returns getpass.getuser() unchanged (src/_pytest/tmpdir.py lines 151–158). This encodes the incorrect assumption that the returned username contains no path separators or other characters that would split into multiple path components on the platform.

2) src/_pytest/tmpdir.py:TempPathFactory.getbasetemp constructs a subdirectory name using the raw username and calls mkdir without creating intermediate parents: it computes rootdir = temproot.joinpath(f"pytest-of-{user}") and calls rootdir.mkdir(exist_ok=True) (src/_pytest/tmpdir.py lines 112–118). This assumes the argument to mkdir is a single-level directory whose parent already exists; if the username contains path separators (for example a Windows domain name containing a backslash, yielding a path like 'pytest-of-contoso/john_doe'), the resulting Path represents nested components and mkdir(exist_ok=True) can raise FileNotFoundError because intermediate parents do not exist.

3) Test coverage gap: testing/test_tmpdir.py contains tests exercising get_user() fallback/None cases (e.g. tests asserting get_user() is None in certain env conditions) but does not include tests that assert behavior when getpass.getuser() contains path separators or other filesystem-unsafe characters, nor tests for Unicode/non-ASCII username handling. Because get_user() is the single upstream source used to construct basetemp (search found its use only in src/_pytest/tmpdir.py and tests), missing tests mean regressions or platform-specific failures can slip in unnoticed (see testing/test_tmpdir.py lines ~228–246).

4) Downstream code compatibility: functions in src/_pytest/pathlib.py that create numbered directories and perform cleanup (make_numbered_dir, make_numbered_dir_with_cleanup, cleanup_numbered_dir) operate on the rootdir Path passed from tmpdir and do not themselves assume a particular username format; they will behave consistently once the rootdir is a valid path. However, they will be affected by the observable failure if the upstream rootdir creation fails.

Notes from investigation (diagnostic observations, not direct prescriptions): the investigation identified a candidate string-normalization pattern for producing a single filesystem-safe component from the raw username (one possible normalization observed during analysis: sanitized = re.sub(r"[^A-Za-z0-9._-]+", "_", raw)). This is provided as an observed normalization candidate to illustrate the relationship required (username -> single safe path component) and not as an obligatory implementation directive. The investigation also found that calling mkdir without creating intermediate parents is fragile when given nested components. Additional evaluation on Windows runtime behavior with embedded separators and on Unicode usernames was not performed and remains an open verification gap.
StepsToReproduce: Create a minimal test file: echo def test_tmpdir(tmpdir):>test_tmp.py && echo   pass>>test_tmp.py
From Windows cmd set an environment username containing a backslash: set LOGNAME=contoso\john_doe
Run pytest: py.test test_tmp.py
Observe the failure when tmpdir/tmpdir_factory attempts to create basetemp (FileNotFoundError referencing a path like '.../pytest-of-contoso/john_doe').
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: pytest should create session base temporary directories for tests (tmpdir/tmpdir_factory/tmp_path_factory) successfully regardless of getpass.getuser() output. The username used in the basetemp directory name should be represented as a single filesystem-safe directory component (i.e., not contain platform path-separator characters or other characters that would cause the path to split into nested components), so directory creation succeeds on Windows and other platforms.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: On Windows, attempting to create the basetemp directory can fail with FileNotFoundError. The error shows the basetemp path split into components (for example '.../Temp/pytest-of-contoso/john_doe'), and pathlib.Path.mkdir raises FileNotFoundError because an intermediate parent directory does not exist.

problem_statement.Title: Misleading CollectError: pytest discourages module-level pytest.skip and hides allow_module_level escape hatch
problem_statement.Description: When pytest encounters a pytest.skip() raised during module import it raises a CollectError with a message that forbids module-level use and suggests pytestmark-based skipping, but that message does not mention the supported escape hatch (allow_module_level=True). This misleads users who try to skip whole modules (for example modules that use newer Python syntax) and results in collection errors instead of a clean module-level skip.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) User-facing guidance in src/_pytest/python.py (Module._importtestmodule) is inconsistent with the skip outcome API. Module._importtestmodule catches skip.Exception and, when the caught exception's allow_module_level attribute is False, raises a CollectError with the message: "Using pytest.skip outside of a test is not allowed. To decorate a test function, use the @pytest.mark.skip or @pytest.mark.skipif decorators instead, and to skip a module use `pytestmark = pytest.mark.{skip,skipif}." (src/_pytest/python.py: lines ~607-615). This message does not mention the existing allow_module_level escape hatch.

2) The outcomes API provides the module-level allowance flag: src/_pytest/outcomes.py defines skip(msg: str = "", *, allow_module_level: bool = False) and raises Skipped(msg=msg, allow_module_level=allow_module_level) (src/_pytest/outcomes.py: lines ~123-145). The existence of this parameter encodes the supported capability that the Module._importtestmodule message omits.

3) Module import handling already respects the skip exception flag: when a Skipped is raised with allow_module_level=True, Module._importtestmodule re-raises the Skipped exception (allowing collection to treat the module as skipped); only when allow_module_level is False does the method raise the CollectError (src/_pytest/python.py: lines ~607-610). The behavioral inconsistency is therefore in the user-facing message, not in the exception/collection handling logic.

4) There is an ergonomics gap: there is no small, obvious public helper in the current outcomes API whose name signals "skip this entire module" (i.e., no dedicated skip_module helper is present in the codebase). While the underlying flag exists, its discoverability and the messaging around top-level skip are insufficient and lead to user confusion (absence observed in src/_pytest/outcomes.py and the public usage patterns in tests).
StepsToReproduce: Create a module pos_only.py that uses Python 3.8-only syntax, e.g. def foo(a, /, b): return a + b

Create a test file test_pos.py with a module-level conditional skip without allow_module_level, e.g.:

import sys
from pytest import skip
if sys.version_info < (3, 8):
    skip(msg='Requires Python >= 3.8')

from pos_only import *

def test_foo():
    assert foo(10, 20) == 30
Run pytest with Python 3.7 (or any <3.8): pytest -q
Observe that collection fails with a CollectError containing the message discouraging module-level pytest.skip
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: User-visible guidance should make it clear that module-level skipping is supported via the existing Skipped.allow_module_level mechanism: when a module intentionally raises pytest.skip(..., allow_module_level=True) during import, collection should record the module as skipped and not fail on module import (e.g. due to newer Python syntax). The user-facing messaging should not incorrectly discourage the module-level use without mentioning the escape hatch or the documented way to allow module-level skips.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Pytest raises a CollectError during module import with the message: "Using pytest.skip outside of a test is not allowed. To decorate a test function, use the @pytest.mark.skip or @pytest.mark.skipif decorators instead, and to skip a module use `pytestmark = pytest.mark.{skip,skipif}." This message discourages module-level pytest.skip and does not mention the existing allow_module_level=True behavior; as a result users attempting to skip modules that require newer Python syntax see collection errors instead of a clean module-level skip.


I think we can drop the `pytestmark` remark from there, it is not skip-specific and passing `allow_module_level` already accomplishes the same.

Maybe this is addressing some past confusion, or we want to push people toward `pytest.mark.skip[if]`, but if we can detect it automatically - we can also deprecate allow_module_level and make `skip()` do the right thing based on the context it's used in.

That's exactly it, people would use `@pytest.skip` instead of `@pytest.mark.skip` and skip the whole module:

Having looked at the links, I think the alternative API to skip a module is more appealing.
Here is a proposed end state:

1. pytest.skip_module is introduced, can be used to skip a module.

problem_statement.Title: pytest prints next statement's decorator line (e.g. "@t") inside assertion failure excerpt on Python 3.9
problem_statement.Description: When an assertion fails, the source excerpt that pytest prints for the failing statement can include the decorator line(s) of the following function (for example, "@t"). This extra decorator line appears in the failure context shown to the user.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1. src/_pytest/_code/source.py::get_statement_startend2 computes statement boundary candidate line numbers by walking the AST and converting AST lineno to 0-based indices using expressions such as x.lineno - 1 and val[0].lineno - 1 - 1 (the latter for orelse/finalbody). (See values.append(x.lineno - 1) and values.append(val[0].lineno - 1 - 1) in get_statement_startend2.)

2. src/_pytest/_code/source.py::getstatementrange_ast uses the start/end values produced by get_statement_startend2, then runs a BlockFinder/tokenize loop which can adjust end, and finally runs a trimming loop that only removes trailing comment lines (line.startswith("#")) or blank lines. There is no special-case trimming for decorator lines (lines starting with "@") before returning source[start:end]. (See the BlockFinder/tokenize usage and the final while loop that checks line.startswith("#") or emptiness.)

3. src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py already special-cases decorated functions by using item.decorator_list[0].lineno in insertion logic ("Special case: for a decorated function, set the lineno to that of the first decorator, not the `def`"), which demonstrates AST/lineno values can point at decorator lines and that decorator-line handling is relevant when mapping AST lineno to source slices.

4. The combination of (1) and (2) — the numeric AST→0-based conversions together with BlockFinder/tokenize adjustments plus the lack of decorator-line trimming — allows a computed end index to align such that a following function's decorator line remains in the returned source[start:end]. Callers that display that slice (for example, src/_pytest/_code/code.py::TracebackEntry.getsource and Source.getstatement) will then show the decorator line in the assertion failure excerpt. In short: the statement-boundary computations and post-processing do not exclude decorator lines, so decorator lines can appear in printed failure contexts.
StepsToReproduce: Run the test with Python 3.9 and pytest 6.2.5: python -m pytest test_statement.py (or your usual pytest invocation using a Python 3.9 interpreter).
Observe the assertion failure output: the decorator line "@t" of the following function appears in the source excerpt for the failing assertion.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When an assertion fails, the displayed source excerpt contains only the lines that belong to the failing statement (the assertion and its immediate relevant lines) and does not include decorator lines that belong to the following function or class.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The assertion failure excerpt can include the decorator line(s) (for example, "@t") from the next function/class in the file in the printed context.
